segments.text: Let me ask you about AI.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: It seems like this year for the entirety of the human civilization is an interesting year for the development of artificial intelligence.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: A lot of interesting stuff is happening.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So Meta is a big part of that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Meta has developed LLAMA, which is a 65 billion parameter model.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: There's a lot of interesting questions they can ask here, one of which has to do with open source.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: But first, can you tell the story of developing of this model and making the complicated decision of how to release it?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Yeah, sure.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: I think you're right, first of all, that in the last year, there have been a bunch of advances on scaling up these large transformer models.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So there's the language equivalent of it with large language models.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: There's sort of the image generation equivalent with these large diffusion models.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: There's a lot of fundamental research that's gone into this.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And Meta has taken the approach
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: of being quite open and academic in our development of AI.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Part of this is we want to have the best people in the world researching this.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And a lot of the best people want to know that they're going to be able to share their work.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So that's part of the deal that we have, is that we can get, if you're one of the top AI researchers in the world, you can come here, you can get access to kind of industry-scale infrastructure.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And part of our ethos is that we want to share what's invented broadly.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: We do that with a lot of the different AI tools that we create.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And Llama is the language model that our research team made.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And we did a limited open source release for it, which was intended for researchers to be able to use it.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: But, you know, responsibility and getting safety right on these is very important.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So we didn't think that for the first one, there were a bunch of questions around whether we should be releasing this commercially.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So we kind of punted on that for V1 of Llama and just released it from research.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Now, obviously, by releasing it for research, you know, it's out there.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: But companies know that they're not supposed to kind of put it into commercial releases.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And, you know, we're working on the follow-up models for this and thinking through how exactly this should work for follow-on now that we've had time to work on a lot more of the safety and the pieces around that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: But overall, I mean, this is,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: I just kind of think that it would be good
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: if there were a lot of different folks who had the ability to build state-of-the-art technology here.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: You know, it's, and not just a small number of big companies.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: But to train one of these AI models, the state-of-the-art models is, you know, it just takes, you know, hundreds of millions of dollars of infrastructure, right?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So there are not that many organizations in the world that can do that at the biggest scale today.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And now it gets more efficient every day.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So I do think that that will be available to more folks over time.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: But I just think like there's all this innovation out there that people can create.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And I just think that we'll also learn a lot by seeing what the whole community of students and hackers and startups and different folks build with this.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And that's kind of been how we've approached this.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And it's also how we've done a lot of our infrastructure.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And we took our whole data center design and our server design, and we built this open compute project where we just made that public.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And part of the theory was like, all right, if we make it so that more people can use the server design, then that'll enable more innovation.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: It'll also make the server design more efficient.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And that'll make our business more efficient too.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So that's worked.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And we've just done this with a lot of our infrastructure.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So for people who don't know, you did the limited release, I think in February of this year of Lama,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: and it got quote unquote leaked,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: meaning like it escaped the limited release aspect.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: But it was, you know, that's something you probably anticipated given that it's just released to researchers.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: We shared it with researchers.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Right, so it's just trying to make sure that there's like a slow release.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Yeah.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: But from there, I just would love to get your comment on what happened next, which is like, there's a very vibrant open source community that just built stuff on top of it.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So there's Lama CPP, basically stuff that makes it more efficient to run on smaller computers.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Yep.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: There's combining with reinforcement learning with human feedback.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So some of the different interesting fine tuning mechanisms.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: There's then also like fine tuning in a GPT three generations.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: There's a lot of GPT for all, Alpaca, Colossal AI, all these kinds of models just kind of spring up like run on top of wood.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Yeah.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Like what do you think about that?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: No, I think it's been really, neat to see.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: I mean, there's been folks who are getting it to run on local devices, right?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So if you're an individual who just wants to experiment with this at home, you probably don't have a large budget to get access to like a large amount of cloud compute.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So getting it to run on your local laptop,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: you know, is pretty good, right?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And pretty relevant.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And then there were things like, yeah, Lama CPP
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: re-implemented it more efficiently.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So, you know, now even when we run our own versions of it, we can do it on way less compute and it just way more efficient, save a lot of money for everyone who uses this.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So that is good.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Um, I do think it's worth calling out that because this was a relatively early release, um, Lama isn't quite as on the frontier as, for example, the biggest open AI models or the biggest, um, Google models, right?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: I mean, you mentioned that the largest Lama model that we released had 65 billion parameters.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And no one knows, you know, I guess, outside of open AI, um, exactly what the specs are for, um, for, for GPT-4.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: But, but I, I think the, you know, my understanding is it's like 10 times bigger.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Um, and I think Google's Palm model is, is also, I think, has about 10 times as many parameters.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Now, the Lama models are very efficient.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So they, they perform well for, for something that's around 65 billion parameters.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So for me, that was also part of this because, you know, there's this whole debate around, you know, is it good for, everyone in the world to have access to, um, to the most frontier AI models.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And I, I think as the AI models start approaching something that's like a super human intelligence, like that, that's a bigger question that we'll have to grapple with.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: But right now, I mean, these are still, you know, very basic tools.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: They're, um, you know, they're, they're powerful in the sense that, you know, a lot of open source software like databases or web servers can enable a lot of pretty important things.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Um, but I don't think anyone looks at the, the, you know, the current generation of Lama and thinks it's, um, you know, anywhere near a super intelligence.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So I think that a bunch of those questions around, like, is it, is it good to, to kind of get out there?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: I think at this stage, surely you, you want more researchers working on it for all the reasons that, um, that open source software has a lot of advantages.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And we talked about efficiency before, but another one is just open source software tends to be more secure because you have more people looking at it openly and scrutinizing it, um, and finding holes in it.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Um, and that makes it more safe.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So I think at this point, it's more, I think it's generally agreed upon that open source software is generally more secure and safer, um, than things that are kind of developed in a silo where people try to get through security through obscurity.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So I think that for the scale of, of, of what we're seeing now with AI, I think we're more likely to get to, you know, good alignment and good, um, understanding of, of, of kind of what needs to do to make this work well by having it be open source.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And, and that's something that I think is, is quite good to have out there and, and, and happening publicly at this point.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Meta released a lot of models as open source.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: So, uh, the massively multilingual speech model, the image model.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Yeah, that was neat.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I mean, I'll, I'll ask you questions about those, but the point is, uh, you've open source quite a lot.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: You've been spearheading the open source movement.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Whereas, um, that's really positive, inspiring to see from one angle, from the research angle, of course, there's folks who are really terrified about the existential threat of artificial intelligence.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And those folks will say that, you know, um, you have to be careful about the open sourcing, uh, step.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: But what, where do you see the future of open source here, uh, as part of meta, the tension here is, do you want to release the magic sauce?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: That's one tension.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And the other one is, uh, do you want to put a powerful tool in the hands of, uh, bad actors, even though it probably has a huge amount of positive impact also?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Yeah.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: I mean, again, I think for the stage that we're at in the development of AI, I don't think anyone looks at the current state of things and thinks that this is super intelligence.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Um, and you know, the models that we're talking about for the llama models here are, you know, generally an order of magnitude smaller than what open AI or Google are doing.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So I think that at least for the stage that we're at now, the equity is balanced strongly in my view towards doing this more openly.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Um, I think if you got something that was closer to super intelligence, then I think you'd have to discuss that more and, and think through that, um, a lot more.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And we haven't made a decision yet as to what we would do if we were in that position, but I don't think, I think there's a good chance that we're pretty far off from that position.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So, um, so I'm, I'm not, uh, I'm certainly not saying that the position that we're taking on this now applies to every single thing that we would ever do.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And, you know, certainly inside the company, you know, we probably do more open source work than, you know, most of the other big tech companies, but we also don't open source everything, right?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And a lot of our, the core kind of app code for WhatsApp or Instagram or something, I mean, we're not, we're not open sourcing that it's not like a, a general enough piece of software that would be useful for a lot of people to do different things.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Um, you know, whereas the software that we do, whether it's like a, an open source server design or, um, or basically, you know, things like memcache, right?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Like a good, you know, it was, it was probably our earliest project, um, that, that I worked on.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: It was probably one of the last things that I, that I coded and, and, and led directly for the company.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Um, but, but basically this like caching tool, um, for, for quick David data retrieval, um, these are things that are just broadly useful across like anything that you want to build.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And, and I think that some of the language models now have that feel as well as some of the other things that we're building, like the translation tool that, that you just referenced.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So text to speech and speech to text, you've expanded it from around a hundred languages to more than 1100 languages.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Yeah.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: And you can identify more than the model can identify more than 4,000 spoken languages, which is 40 times more than any known previous technology.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: To me, that's really, really, really exciting in terms of connecting the world, breaking down barriers that language creates.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Yeah.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: I think being able to translate between all of these different pieces in real time, this has been a
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: kind of common sci-fi idea that we'd all have, you know, whether it's, I don't know, an earbud or glasses or something that can help translate in real time, um, between all these different languages.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And that's one that I think technology is basically
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: delivering now.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So I think, yeah, I think that's pretty, pretty exciting.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Uh, you mentioned the next version of Lama.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: What can you say about the next version of Lama?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: What, what, what can you say about like, what, uh, what were you working on in terms of release in terms of the vision for that?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: Well, a lot of what we're doing is taking the first version, which was primarily, you know, this research version and trying to now build a version that has all of the latest state-of-the-art safety precautions built in.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Um, and, and we're, um, we're using some more data to train it, um, from across our services, but, but a lot of the, the work that we're doing internally is really just focused on making sure that this is, um, you know, as aligned and responsible as, as possible.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: And, you know, we're building a lot of our own, you know, we're talking about kind of the open source infrastructure, but, you know, the, the main thing that we focus on building here, you know, a lot of product experience is to help people connect and express themselves.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So, you know, we're gonna, I've, I've talked about a bunch of this stuff, but, um, then you'll have, you know, an assistant that you can talk to in WhatsApp.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Um, you know, I think, I think in the future, every creator will, will have kind of an AI agent that can kind of act on their behalf that their fans can talk to.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: I, I want to get to the point where every small business basically has an AI agent that people can talk to for, you know, to do commerce and customer support and things like that.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So there are going to be all these different things and LAMA or the language model underlying this is, is basically going to be the engine that powers that the reason to open source it is that, um, as, as we did with, um, with the first version is that it, uh, you know, basically it, it unlocks a lot of innovation in the ecosystem will, will make our products better as well.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Um, and also gives us a lot of valuable feedback on security and safety, which is important for making this good, but yeah, I mean the, the, the work that we're doing to advance the infrastructure it's, um, it's basically at this point taking it beyond a research project into something which is ready to be kind of core infrastructure, not only for our own products, but, um, you know, hopefully for, for a lot of other things out there too.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Do you think the LAMA or
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: This is, I mean, we were talking about the debates that we have internally and I think, um, I think the question is how to do it, right?
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: I mean, it's, I think we,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: you know, we, we did the research license for V1 and, and I think the, the, the big thing that we're, that we're thinking about is, is basically like what's the, what's the right, the right way.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: So there was a leak that happened.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.text: I don't know if you can comment on it for V1.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: It's like, you don't have any reputable companies that are basically trying to put this into, into, um, their commercial products, but, but yeah, but by sharing it with, you know, so many researchers, it's, it's, you know, it did leave the building.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: But, uh, what have you learned from that process that you might be able to apply to V2 about how to release it safely,
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_01

segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00

segments.text: Yeah.
segments.speaker: SPEAKER_00
